meaning: cannot afford to; must not; unable to
detailed_explanation: Expresses that an action is impossible due to circumstances, obligations, or social constraints. Implies a sense of duty or external pressure preventing the action.
conjugation_rules: Verb (dictionary form) + わけにはいかない

usage_examples.japanese: 仕事があるから、遊びに行くわけにはいかない
usage_examples.reading: しごとがあるから、あそびにいくわけにはいかない
usage_examples.meaning: I have work, so I cannot afford to go out and play
usage_examples.nuance: Expresses obligation preventing the action - responsibility blocks it
usage_examples.context: Telling friends you can't hang out because of work
usage_examples.alternative: 〜なければならない (if expressing obligation instead)

usage_examples.japanese: お客様の前で、怒るわけにはいかない
usage_examples.reading: おきゃくさまのまえで、おこるわけにはいかない
usage_examples.meaning: In front of customers, I must not get angry

nuance_notes: This pattern implies external circumstances make an action impossible. It's often used for: (1) Moral obligations (2) Professional duties (3) Social harmony (4) Economic constraints. The speaker WANTS to do it but CANNOT.

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜わけにはいかない = cannot afford to (want but can't); 〜わけがない = no way that (strong denial)
〜わけではない = doesn't mean that (explanation/clarification)

common_mistakes: Don't confuse with 〜わけがない (strong denial). Note: This is about 'I want to but can't' not 'there's no way'. Also: you CANNOT use this with adjectives directly (must use 〜くて/〜で form first for adjectives).

meaning: no way that; impossible that; there's no reason
detailed_explanation: Strong denial or disbelief. Expresses that something is impossible or unthinkable based on logic or evidence.
conjugation_rules: Verb/い-adjective (plain form) / な-adjective + な / Noun + の + わけがない

usage_examples.reading: かれがぬすむわけがない
usage_examples.meaning: There's no way he would steal
usage_examples.nuance: Strong denial based on character knowledge

usage_examples.japanese: あの子が犯人のわけがない
usage_examples.reading: あのこがはんにんのわけがない
usage_examples.meaning: There's no way that child is the criminal

nuance_notes: This expresses logical IMPOSSIBILITY or strong disbelief. It's NOT about wanting - it's about the situation being unthinkable. Common in defense, deduction, denying accusations.

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜わけがない = no way (logical denial); 〜わけにはいかない = cannot afford to (want but prevented)
〜はずがない = should not be (expectation-based); 〜わけがない = no way (logic-based)

common_mistakes: Confused with 〜わけにはいかない (opposite meaning!). わけがない = denial. わけにはいかない = cannot do (despite wanting).

meaning: it doesn't mean that; not necessarily; it's not that
detailed_explanation: Clarification or partial denial. Softens a statement or corrects a misunderstanding without full negation.
conjugation_rules: Verb/い-adjective (plain form) / な-adjective + な / Noun + な + わけではない

usage_examples.japanese: 嫌いなわけではない、ただ時間がない
usage_examples.reading: きらいなわけではない、ただじかんがない
usage_examples.meaning: It's not that I dislike it, I just don't have time

usage_examples.japanese: できないわけではない
usage_examples.reading: できないわけではない
usage_examples.meaning: It's not that I can't do it
usage_examples.alternative: 〜できることはない (stronger 'it's not impossible')

nuance_notes: This pattern CLARIFIES - it doesn't fully deny, it adjusts. Often used to: (1) Decline politely (2) Correct misunderstandings (3) Soften statements. Usually followed by 〜 (the real reason).

related_patterns.key_difference: More explanatory, often used to summarize
〜わけではない = partial denial/clarification; 〜わけがない = strong denial

common_mistakes: Don't use for strong denial - that's 〜わけがない. This is softer clarification. Also: な follows な-adjectives and nouns here (not の).

meaning: should; ought to; expressing emotion; general truth
detailed_explanation: Expresses emotional responses, general truths, common sense, or soft advice. Connects to shared human experience.
conjugation_rules: Verb (plain past/present) + ものだ / もんだ (casual)

usage_examples.japanese: 時間が経つのは早いものだ
usage_examples.reading: じかんがたつのははやいものだ
usage_examples.nuance: Expresses sentiment about universal truth - nostalgic

usage_examples.japanese: 親には感謝するものだ
usage_examples.reading: おやにはかんしゃするものだ
usage_examples.meaning: One should be grateful to parents

nuance_notes: Multiple functions: (1) Emotional response to universal truths (2) General/common-sense advice (3) Soft obligation. Softer than 〜べきだ. Often used with 〜は for general statements.

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜ものだ = general truth/emotion; 〜ものではない = strong prohibition (should never)
〜べきだ = stronger obligation; 〜ものだ = softer/common-sense

common_mistakes: Often confused with 〜ものではない (prohibition vs. truth). もんだ is casual contraction, not a different grammar point. Context determines if emotional or advisory.

detailed_explanation: Expresses logical expectation or supposition based on evidence, rules, or normal circumstances.
conjugation_rules: Verb/い-adjective (plain form) + はず / な-adjective + な + はず / Noun + の + はず

usage_examples.japanese: 電車は10時に来るはずだ
usage_examples.reading: でんしゃはじゅうじにくるはずだ
usage_examples.meaning: The train should come at 10 o'clock

usage_examples.japanese: 彼はもう知っているはずだ
usage_examples.reading: かれはもうしっているはずだ
usage_examples.alternative: 〜ことになっている (more definite plan)

nuance_notes: This is about EXPECTATION - 'based on X, Y should happen'. Different from 〜べき (obligation). Also used in negative: 〜はずがない (no way).
common_mistakes: Don't confuse with 〜べき (obligation). はず = what you expect. Also: 〜はずだ for predictions, 〜はずだった for counterfactual (failed expectation).

detailed_explanation: Expresses moral obligation, duty, or strong recommendation. What one ought to do from ethical or rational standpoint.
conjugation_rules: Verb (dictionary form) + べき / する → すべき

usage_examples.reading: やくそくはまもるべきだ

usage_examples.japanese: 親は子供を大切にすべきだ
usage_examples.reading: おやはこどもをたいせつにすべきだ

nuance_notes: This is about OBLIGATION/DUTY - 'it is right to do'. Stronger than 〜ものだ (general advice) or 〜たらいい (suggestion). Note: する becomes すべき (irregular).

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜なければならない = stronger duty/often external obligation; 〜べき = moral/should
〜たらいい = casual suggestion; 〜べき = strong recommendation/obligation

common_mistakes: Confused with 〜はず (expectation vs. obligation). Also: する → すべき (not するべき). Negative is rare (〜ないべき), usually rephrased.

meaning: about to; just finished; in the process of
detailed_explanation: Temporal anchor - indicates timing relative to an action: about to start, just finished, or currently happening.
conjugation_rules: Verb (dictionary form) + ところ = about to / Verb (te-form) + いるところ = in progress / Verb (past) + ところ = just finished

usage_examples.japanese: 今、食べているところだ
usage_examples.reading: いま、たべているところだ
usage_examples.meaning: Now, I'm in the middle of eating

nuance_notes: Three main uses: (1) 〜るところ = about to (not yet started) (2) 〜ているところ = in middle of (3) 〜たところ = just finished. Perfect for describing timing of interruptions.

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜ばかり = just finished/recent; 〜たところ = exact moment of completion
〜たばかり emphasizes recency; 〜たところ emphasizes the moment

common_mistakes: Mixing up forms: dictionary form = about to, te-form+iru = in progress, past = just finished. Also: 〜たところで (different meaning - even after doing).

detailed_explanation: Expresses intention or plan. What the speaker plans to do (or not do).

usage_examples.japanese: 来年日本へ行くつもりだ
usage_examples.reading: らいねんにほんへいくつもりだ
usage_examples.meaning: I intend to go to Japan next year

usage_examples.japanese: お酒を飲まないつもりだ
usage_examples.reading: おさけをのまないつもりだ
usage_examples.meaning: I intend not to drink alcohol

nuance_notes: About personal INTENTION - not prediction or expectation. Often contrasted with 〜ようと思う (thinking of doing - less firm).

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜ようと思う = thinking of doing (less firm); 〜つもり = clear intention

common_mistakes: Not the same as 〜ようと思う (thinking about it) or 〜予定 (scheduled). つもり implies firm intention. Past tense 〜つもりだった = intended but didn't.

detailed_explanation: Expresses contradiction or unexpected result. Something happened despite conditions that should have prevented it.
conjugation_rules: Verb/い-adjective (plain form) / な-adjective + な / Noun + な + のに

usage_examples.japanese: 雨が降っているのに、出かけた
usage_examples.reading: あめがふっているのに、でかけた
usage_examples.meaning: Even though it was raining, (I/he) went out

usage_examples.reading: かんたんなのに、できない
usage_examples.meaning: Even though it's simple, I can't do it

nuance_notes: This carries EMOTION - surprise, frustration, disappointment. The result contradicts expectations. Different from 〜けれど (simple contrast without emotion).
common_mistakes: Don't confuse with 〜のために (for the purpose of). Also: のに carries emotion - if just stating contrast without feeling, use 〜けれど. な follows な-adjectives and nouns here.
